Hey, welcome back. Okay, we're in a great spot here. You just think you're intelligence gathering questions. You got the first part of your script done. Let's move on. Now we are in the main body of the presentation, which is pretty much where the rubber meets the road. Now, when I go to a company, what I do typically is I'll have sales force get together. I'll say, right guys, I want you to work together as a group and I want you to come up with the five best language patterns. Each of you come up with your own five best language patterns for the product, five best language patterns for yourself, five best language patterns for the company. So for each of the three 10s, and I'm going to pull them all together and we'll pick out the absolute best of the best. And I'll show you how to then put those into one cohesive script, main body, right? Now let me give you some direct point this year because there are seven things that I can give you right now that are sort of these benchmarks to take you through this process step by step to make sure you get it right, you know, as quickly as possible, right? So here's the number one we start off with when you go into the main body. So remember we go with the transition says, you know, Jim Jill, based on what you said to me, this is a perfect fit for you. Let me explain why. So now in the case of let's say the movie, the Wolf of Wall Street, what you have is when we go name of the company, Ayrton International. It is a cutting edge, high tech firm out of the Midwest. So in other words, that first thing you do is you say, name your company or name of your product or your processes, right? And then you follow it up by a quick explanation that literally just kicks butt here. In other words, you want it to sound great. It's short. It's sweet. It's intense. It makes a lot of it catches the attention. Let me go through the Wolf of Wall Street one more time. Here's what Leo said. Name of the company, Ayrton International. It's a cutting edge, high tech firm out of the Midwest waiting imminent patent approval on a next generation of radar detectors that have both huge military and civilian applications. That was what he said, right? Reason to know as well as I wrote it. Now just so you know, the amount of time it took me to write that sentence almost a day believe it or not. Now, obviously I took extra times. I knew it would be a movie, right? Now bigger movie, but I knew it would be a movie, right? So I really took extra care of that. When Leo said those words, it obeyed every rule. We just went through it creating scripts. It wasn't no tongue twisters. It flowed. It was powerful. It sounded great. Remember, this is about the truth. Well told. Like you could say, you know, Ayrton International makes rail detectors or it's a cutting edge, high tech firm out of the Midwest. It's just boom, beholding, right? It just sounds great. Now remember, you gotta be honest and ethical here, but I want you to position your product or good or your service as sexy and as powerful as possible, right? And then from there, step two is what you do now is you're gonna focus on one particular feature and then expand on that with the benefits. Now typically for most people, you'll have sort of, you'll pretty much know what your main benefits are and that pretty much you're gonna match most people. But if you're one of those industry where everything's very different, try to be fluent with this party of scripts. Try to match it as closely as possible to their primary needs and their pain points. Okay? Don't be so generic about it, right? Now move on to step three. Step three is where you get a check in with your prospect. In other words, you don't just keep going on. You say, you follow me so far or make sense. See, if you don't check in with your prospect here, what will happen? A, probably start tuning out because you should talk, talk, talk, you're talking ahead. And also you wanna make sure that he does get you or she gets you so far. In other words, if you say to someone, you follow me so far, they say, no, I don't. You don't just plow on, that breaks rapport and so does you don't care, right? So what would you do? You say, oh, well, you back up and you restate the same information in a more succinct way and then say, does that make sense? I say, oh yeah, it's a great. Now just to be clear, I've done this for 35 years. I think it'd be once or twice. Someone said, no, it doesn't make sense because you're not using things that require a rocket science degree here. It's basic stuff here. But what you're doing is you're showing someone that you care if they understand, you give them a chance to catch their breath and make sure it's a two-way dialogue versus a one-way monologue, right? So that's the end of step three. We stop at six, two, step four and step four is guess what? You're going to repeat step two and three. Now, so you don't want to just have one feature in the main body. We want to pick out two key features and expand on both features, lots of benefits. But we hook those together, separated in the middle out by that third thing, which is you follow me so far. You get it? So that's what you do after you say you follow me so far. Then you go, he says, great. You say, great. Now, next benefit, boom, and you go there. Okay? So you're done with that second benefit. You'll say you follow me so far again. They'll say, yeah, great. And then guess what we do from there? We're actually going to repeat that process one more time. And the reason for that is if you have just two features and benefits in there, it's probably going to seem a bit thin. Now again, this has to be customized based on your own business. So you got to be smart here. But the point is in most cases, I like to use a stack of three features and obviously then three strings of benefits that are derived from those features. And by doing this, that one, two, three punch and you are set. But of course, what we don't do is you don't go on and on with a thousand and one features and a thousand one benefits. That's going to be massively front loading and will shoot us in the foot in a massive way. Now, in truth, if you want to know why a lot of people in the sales world do this, they just go on and on is because they don't know the straight line and they're scared, almost fearful to get to the point where they end their presentation and they get to that dreaded moment that they ask for the order and get a, let me think about it. And everyone's scared of that. But we know with the straight line that simply when the sale begins, we're going to roll up our sleeves and go to work with the looping process. And there you have it. And then from there now, we want to step number five, which is as you transition into the close, you want to create urgency wherever possible. Remember, urgency is about buying the product or service now. So if there's some way that you can create urgency, if your product is in short supply, maybe there's a price increase around the corner, a new model coming out, some something that allows you to say there's a reason to buy now because it's in short supply or time is running out or the product is going on first, whatever it might be. Now don't lie. You don't want to manufacture artificial bullshit urgency here. But what I want you to do is if you own your own company, you should be building urgency into your offers. That's something you can, it's like you can create urgency by putting a price increase down the road or changing the model number and you can create scarcity legitimately and great companies always do. It's why Apple always has. Notice there's only X amount of phones in the launch, then you're on a waiting list. They create scarcity, increases demand, boom. So it's an ethical thing to do and it works like a charm. So you always want to create urgency wherever possible. And then moving on from there, number six, here's an important one. Remember that overarching equation, energy in, benefits out. You're not just going to go in and say, getting started is really complicated. You're going to go back to what we said. Now, Jim, so you create that urgency. So you know, Jim, Jill, getting started is very simple. It's just a question of your name, basic information, whatever is specific to your industry. Just remember, now you don't want to lie and say something is simple if it's not. In some industries like, you know, mortgages or banging, there are some hoops that the prospect has to jump through to open up an account. But what you want to get across here is that despite whatever those hoops are, you're going to be there to make it as easy as possible. And you're not going to make it more complex because you're just like, you know, making it difficult for the clients. You don't know what you're doing. You understand? You want to understand is whatever hoops there are, you're going to help them. You're going to shorten the process as much as possible and make it very simple. Energy in, benefits out. Doing that right, you set yourself up a massive greatness. And now we move on to the next one, number seven. This is a simple one. You want to directly ask for the order. Why am I saying that with such passion? Because the oddity of sales is that untrained salespeople, especially novices, they're like scared to ask for the order. They was dense around it. They say everything, but they don't just say, you know, here's what you should be doing right now. Pick up X number, you know, buy, they don't do it. Ask for the order directly, politely. They say, you know, John, pick up X number, believe me, you'll be very, very impressed. Sound good. And just shut up. You ask for the order, not with pressure, but calmly, coolly. So I'm going to give you one shot and believe me, the only problem you'll have is that you didn't buy more. Sound fair enough? These old men, simple as that. Okay. And with that, I want to stop for a moment here. I want you to take a quiz, lock this stuff in, and as soon as you're done, meet me back in the next lesson and I'll be there waiting for you. See you soon.
